title: Chemical Bonding and VSEPR Theory
subject: AP Chemistry
objectives: Distinguish between ionic, covalent, and metallic bonding based on electronegativity differences
Draw Lewis structures and use VSEPR theory to predict molecular geometry
Determine whether a molecule is polar or nonpolar based on geometry and bond polarity

sections.heading: Three Types of Chemical Bonds
sections.content:
Chemical bonds form because atoms are more stable when bonded than when alone. There are three main types:

Ionic bonds form between metals and nonmetals when one atom transfers electrons to another. The resulting positive and negative ions attract each other electrostatically. NaCl is the classic example: sodium loses one electron to become Na⁺, chlorine gains it to become Cl⁻, and the electrostatic attraction holds them in a crystal lattice.

Covalent bonds form between nonmetals when atoms share electrons. In H₂, each hydrogen contributes one electron to a shared pair, giving both atoms a filled 1s shell. Covalent bonds can be single (one shared pair), double (two shared pairs), or triple (three shared pairs).

Metallic bonds are found in pure metals. Valence electrons are delocalized — not belonging to any single atom but shared across a 'sea' of electrons that holds the positive metal cation cores together. This explains why metals conduct electricity, are malleable, and have luster.

sections.heading: Lewis Structures and VSEPR Geometry
sections.content:
A Lewis structure shows how valence electrons are distributed in a molecule. To draw one: (1) count total valence electrons, (2) connect atoms with single bonds, (3) complete octets on outer atoms, (4) place remaining electrons on the central atom, (5) convert lone pairs to double/triple bonds if the central atom lacks an octet.

VSEPR (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion) theory predicts molecular geometry: electron groups around the central atom arrange themselves to minimize repulsion.

2 electron groups → linear (180°)
3 electron groups → trigonal planar (120°)
4 electron groups → tetrahedral (109.5°)
5 electron groups → trigonal bipyramidal (90°/120°)
6 electron groups → octahedral (90°)

Lone pairs count as electron groups but are not visible in the molecular shape. This creates bent, trigonal pyramidal, seesaw, T-shaped, and square planar geometries depending on the combination of bonding pairs and lone pairs.

sections.heading: Water: Why It's Bent, Not Linear
sections.content:
Water (H₂O): Oxygen has 6 valence electrons. Two are shared with hydrogens (two bonding pairs), and four remain as two lone pairs.

Electron geometry: 4 electron groups around oxygen → tetrahedral arrangement.
Molecular geometry: 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs → bent shape.
Bond angle: ~104.5° (slightly less than 109.5° because lone pairs take up more space than bonding pairs and squeeze the bonds closer together).

Because water is bent (not linear) and the O−H bonds are polar (oxygen is more electronegative), the bond dipoles do NOT cancel. Water is a polar molecule. If water were linear, the dipoles would cancel, it would be nonpolar, and life as we know it would not exist.

VSEPR predicts geometry by minimizing electron group repulsion. Lone pairs compress bond angles. A molecule is polar only if it has polar bonds AND a geometry where the dipoles do not cancel. Symmetrical molecules (like CO₂ and CH₄) can have polar bonds but be nonpolar overall.

sections.heading: Intermolecular Forces: What Holds Molecules Together
sections.content:
Bonds hold atoms together within a molecule. Intermolecular forces (IMFs) hold molecules to each other. IMFs are much weaker than covalent bonds, but they determine physical properties like boiling point, melting point, and solubility.

London Dispersion Forces (LDF): Present in ALL molecules. Caused by temporary, instantaneous dipoles from fluctuating electron distributions. Strength increases with molecular size (more electrons = more polarizable). This is why larger noble gases and longer hydrocarbons have higher boiling points.

Dipole-Dipole Forces: Present in polar molecules. The positive end of one molecule attracts the negative end of another. Stronger than LDF of similar-sized molecules.

Hydrogen Bonding: A special, strong type of dipole-dipole interaction. Occurs when H is bonded to F, O, or N (very electronegative, small atoms) and interacts with a lone pair on F, O, or N of a neighboring molecule. Hydrogen bonding explains water's unusually high boiling point, surface tension, and specific heat.

For the AP exam, you need to identify which IMFs are present in a substance and use them to predict and explain physical properties. The ranking is: ion-ion > hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole > London dispersion (for molecules of similar size).

aiContext: AP Chemistry. Use dimensional analysis for all calculations. Stress WHY reactions happen, not just equation balancing. Common confusion: entropy vs enthalpy direction, and applying Le Chatelier to specific perturbations.
